pmos

Data Engineering — Primer

Created using pmos‑learnkit

Primer · Cross-Functional Skills. The data warehouse gets the glory, but it would be empty without the unglamorous plumbing that fills it. Behind every fresh dashboard is a chain of moving parts that copies data out of a dozen source systems, cleans and reshapes it, runs it on a schedule, and checks it didn't break — all before an analyst writes a single query. That plumbing is data engineering, and when a metric is stale, wrong, or "down," this is almost always where it happened. This primer is a deep, plain-English tour for product managers who consume data but have never seen how it gets delivered: what data engineering is and the lifecycle that frames it; what a data pipeline actually is; how data is pulled in (ingestion and change data capture); the ETL-versus-ELT choice; batch versus streaming; transformation; orchestration (the conductor); data quality and testing; observability and data contracts; the engineer's toolkit and DataOps; what it all means for you; and the costs, failure modes, and pitfalls. Every term of art is defined on first use and every acronym is spelled out. Source provenance: curation-consensus across the data-engineering canon — the field's anchor text *Fundamentals of Data Engineering* (Reis & Housley, O'Reilly), vendor explainers from Databricks, Fivetran, Airbyte, Striim, Dagster, ZenML, Integrate.io, Matillion, RudderStack, and DataCamp, and the data-reliability writing of Monte Carlo and OneUptime. 17 verified sources.

What data engineering is

Data engineering is the discipline of building the systems that move data from where it is created to where it can be used. If a data analyst answers questions and a data scientist builds models, the data engineer is the one who makes sure clean, reliable, well-organized data is sitting there waiting for them. As Xebia summarizes the canonical text, the data engineer's job is to build infrastructure that prepares data "for further analysis by data analysts and scientist" — in other words, to serve downstream consumers. (Xebia)

The field's anchor reference is *Fundamentals of Data Engineering* by Joe Reis and Matt Housley (O'Reilly, 2022). Their central move was to stop defining the field by its tools — which change every two years — and define it instead by a repeatable process. That process is the data engineering lifecycle, and it is the single most useful mental model in this whole primer.

For a product manager, the one-sentence version is this: data engineering is the supply chain for your numbers. Raw material (events, transactions, records) comes in at one end; a finished product (a trustworthy table an analyst can query) comes out the other. Everything in this primer is a station on that supply chain.

The data engineering lifecycle

Reis and Housley frame the entire field as a five-stage lifecycle. As Xebia puts it, the lifecycle comprises the stages that "turn raw data into a useful end product." (Xebia) The official book description lists the five stages as generation, storage, ingestion, transformation, and serving data. (O'Reilly)

Crucially, the lifecycle also names what Reis and Housley call "undercurrents" — concerns that cut across every stage rather than living in one box. The official description lists six: security, data management, DataOps, data architecture, orchestration, and software engineering. (O'Reilly) The reason this matters: things like data quality and orchestration are not a single step you do once; they are continuous responsibilities that apply at ingestion, at transformation, and at serving alike. Several sections below are really just one undercurrent examined up close.

What a data pipeline actually is

If the lifecycle is the map, a data pipeline is the vehicle that drives the route. Matillion defines it as "a series of data processing steps that move data from one system to another." (Matillion) That is the whole idea: a pipeline picks data up in one place and puts it down, changed or unchanged, in another — automatically and on a repeating basis.

People often say "pipeline" and "ETL" as if they are the same thing, but they are not. ETL — extract, transform, load — is one kind of pipeline, the kind that reshapes data before delivering it. Matillion notes a general data pipeline is broader and "may skip the transformation phase entirely, simply moving data between systems as-is." (Matillion) RudderStack pins down the ETL variety: "a data processing workflow that extracts data from one or more source systems, transforms it into a structured and consistent format, and loads it into a target system." (RudderStack)

Dagster breaks a pipeline into its working parts: extraction "focusing on retrieving relevant data from various sources," transformation that "refines raw data into structured formats suitable for analysis," loading that moves data "into the target system," and orchestration, which is "pivotal to maintaining order and efficiency." (Dagster) The defining feature for a PM to remember is automation: as RudderStack puts it, "as new data is created or updated, the pipeline automatically extracts, transforms, and loads it to the target system." (RudderStack) Nobody re-runs these by hand — that is the point.

Ingestion: getting the data in

Ingestion is the first real step of the pipeline: copying data out of the source systems where it is born — your app's database, your payment processor, your support tool, your ad platforms — and landing it somewhere central. The naïve way is to copy everything every night. The smart way only copies what changed, and that technique has a name product managers will hear constantly: change data capture.

Fivetran defines change data capture (CDC) as "a mechanism for identifying and capturing data changes" that "automatically deliver[s] changes that are detected from a data source (such as a database) to a target (such as a cloud data warehouse)." (Fivetran) Instead of re-copying a hundred-million-row table nightly, CDC ships only the rows that were inserted, updated, or deleted since last time. Fivetran's point is that this lets organizations "capture data on a faster and timelier cadence" because incremental updates save enormous time and resources.

How does CDC know what changed? Usually by reading the database's own transaction log — the running diary every database keeps of every write. Airbyte explains that the most reliable approach is "log-based implementation, where the database transaction log is examined for changes," often using an open-source tool called Debezium that streams each insert, update, and delete downstream. (Airbyte) In practice most teams do not build this themselves; they rent a managed connector (Fivetran, Airbyte) that handles the source databases, schema changes, and retries for them. When a PM hears "we get Salesforce data through Fivetran," this is the machinery being described.

ETL vs ELT: when to transform

Both ETL and ELT use the same three verbs — extract, transform, load — but in a different order, and that ordering is one of the defining debates of modern data engineering. In the old way, ETL (extract, transform, load), you cleaned and reshaped data on a separate machine before loading the tidy result into the warehouse. In the new way, ELT (extract, load, transform), you load the raw data into the warehouse first and reshape it there.

Databricks defines ELT as "a modern data integration approach designed for cloud-native analytics platforms," where data "is loaded before transformation—the opposite of traditional ETL." (Databricks) The order flipped for two concrete reasons Databricks names: cloud warehouses brought "elastic, massively parallel compute capabilities that made in-warehouse transformations practical," and cheap cloud storage "made retaining large volumes of raw data economically feasible." (Databricks) When transforming inside the warehouse became fast and storing raw data became cheap, doing the heavy lifting beforehand on a separate box stopped making sense.

Why a PM should care about the order: under ELT, the raw data is already sitting in the warehouse, so when a definition changes — "actually, let's count trial users differently" — the team can re-transform from raw without rebuilding the whole ingestion pipeline. That flexibility is why ELT now dominates, though ETL still wins when data must be cleaned or anonymized before it is allowed to land (think sensitive personal data).

Batch vs streaming: how often the data moves

A second big choice is timing: does the pipeline run on a clock, or continuously? Striim draws the line cleanly. Batch processing "collects data over time and processes it in scheduled chunks," with latency of "hours to days." Stream processing "handles data continuously the moment it arrives," with "sub-second to seconds" latency. (Striim)

The trade-off is freshness against complexity and cost. Striim notes batch is "lower" complexity with "predictable" cost (scheduled compute windows that shut down when done), while streaming is "higher" complexity — "ordering, exactly-once semantics, state management" — running on "continuous, always-on infrastructure." (Striim) Batch suits end-of-day reporting and large backfills; streaming earns its keep for fraud detection, live operational dashboards, and alerting. And it is not either/or — "Most modern architectures combine both approaches." (Striim) For a PM, the practical signal: if you ask for a "real-time" metric, you are asking the team to move from cheap batch to expensive streaming — a real cost decision, not a toggle.

Transformation: shaping raw data into something usable

Raw ingested data is rarely usable as-is: timestamps are in five formats, the same customer appears under three IDs, and a dozen tables need joining before a single metric makes sense. Transformation is the stage that fixes this. As Dagster describes it, transformation "refines raw data into structured formats suitable for analysis and business intelligence." (Dagster) BI (Business Intelligence) is just the umbrella term for dashboards and reporting.

In the modern ELT world this work happens inside the warehouse, and the dominant tool is dbt (data build tool), where engineers write transformations as SQL (Structured Query Language) select statements — the same query language analysts already use. The output of this stage is the clean, modeled, documented tables that everything downstream depends on. This is also where the most consequential business logic lives: the definition of "active user," the rules for what counts as revenue, the logic that stitches sessions together. When two dashboards disagree, the cause is almost always two different transformations encoding two different definitions — which is exactly why testing this stage, covered next, matters so much.

Orchestration: the conductor

A real data platform is not one pipeline; it is hundreds, and many depend on each other — you cannot transform a table until it has been ingested, and you cannot serve a dashboard until the transform finishes. Orchestration is what coordinates all of it. ZenML describes orchestration tools as "conductors for your tech environment, automating the setup, coordination, and management of your systems." (ZenML)

The central concept is the DAG — a Directed Acyclic Graph. It is less intimidating than it sounds: a map of tasks with arrows showing what must run before what, with no loops allowed (nothing can depend on itself). ZenML notes DAGs let teams "define complex dependencies and task relationships." (ZenML) The orchestrator reads the DAG, runs each task in the right order, retries the ones that fail, and raises an alarm when something stays broken.

Integrate.io draws the useful distinction between orchestration and plain ETL: "Data orchestration manages the entire workflow of data processes across an enterprise, while ETL focuses specifically on extracting, transforming, and loading." Orchestration adds "dynamic dependency management," event-driven triggers, and "sophisticated recovery mechanisms," coordinating "multiple data processes across disparate systems." (Integrate.io) The three best-known orchestrators reflect different philosophies, per ZenML: Airflow is task-centric, Dagster is asset-centric ("assets are first-class citizens"), and Prefect prioritizes developer experience. (ZenML) Which one a team uses is genuinely debated and depends on whether they care most about ecosystem maturity, data lineage, or speed of development.

Data quality and testing

A pipeline that runs perfectly but delivers wrong numbers is worse than one that fails loudly — because nobody knows to distrust it. Data testing is the first line of defense. DataCamp explains that in dbt, "tests are SQL-based assertions that validate data assumptions," running automatically before data reaches users. (DataCamp)

The four built-in checks are intuitive once named: not_null ("ensures a column does not contain any NULL values" — NULL is the database's word for "no value here"), unique ("values are distinct across rows," used for IDs), accepted_values ("a predefined set of allowed values," e.g. status must be one of active/churned/paused), and relationships ("validates referential integrity between tables"). (DataCamp) Their value, in DataCamp's words, is to "detect data issues early — before downstream dashboards and reports are affected." (DataCamp)

But testing has a ceiling, and this is a genuinely contested point. Monte Carlo argues a test only catches problems you thought to write a test for: "If a test isn't written ahead of time to cover that specific incident, an alert isn't set," and bluntly, "Data testing is not feasible at scale." (Monte Carlo) That gap — the unknown problems no one anticipated — is the case for observability, the next section. (Note Monte Carlo sells an observability product, so weigh the framing accordingly; the underlying point about test coverage is widely accepted.)

Observability and data contracts

Where testing checks for known problems, data observability watches for unknown ones. Monte Carlo defines it as "full visibility into the health of your data and systems so you are the first to know when the data is wrong, what broke, and how to fix it," and names the thing it prevents — "data downtime" — as "periods of time when data is partial, erroneous, missing, or otherwise inaccurate." (Monte Carlo) Its five pillars are freshness, quality, volume, schema, and lineage, the last of which "tells you which upstream sources and downstream ingestors were impacted." (Monte Carlo) Lineage is what lets an engineer trace a broken dashboard back to the one source table that changed.

The complementary idea is the data contract — a formal agreement that stops breakages at the source. Monte Carlo defines it as "an agreement between a service provider and data consumers": "a set of defined rules and technical measures that automatically enforce how data should look and behave," specifying "the exact schema a service will output, the valid range of values for specific fields, and the expected refresh frequency." (Monte Carlo) The problem they solve is one PMs cause more often than they realize: when a producing team "make[s] an update to their service that results in a schema change, these tightly coupled data systems crash." (Monte Carlo) An SLA (Service-Level Agreement) — a promise about freshness or uptime — is often part of such a contract. When an engineer asks you to "respect the data contract" before renaming an event, this is why.

The toolkit and DataOps

Pull the pieces together and you have a recognizable stack: ingestion connectors (Fivetran, Airbyte) feed a warehouse or lake; dbt transforms inside it; an orchestrator (Airflow, Dagster, Prefect) schedules the whole chain; and observability tools watch it. The practices that keep this reliable are collectively called DataOps — one of the lifecycle undercurrents — borrowing the automate-test-monitor discipline of software engineering and applying it to data.

The most important DataOps idea for a PM to understand is idempotency. Airbyte defines it as ensuring "operations produce identical results whether executed once or multiple times." (Airbyte) That sounds abstract until you hit a failure: a pipeline dies halfway, you re-run it, and now half the rows are duplicated. An idempotent pipeline can be safely re-run because, as Airbyte puts it, "Idempotent operations allow systems to safely retry failed operations without worrying about duplicate processing." (Airbyte) OneUptime gives the plain version: "An idempotent pipeline produces the same result whether it runs once or ten times for the same input," typically achieved by overwriting a whole day's partition rather than appending. (OneUptime) This is also what makes backfills — re-running a pipeline over past dates to fix or add history — safe to do.

What this means for product managers

You will not build pipelines, but understanding them changes three things. First, it explains why data is sometimes stale or wrong: a pipeline lagged, an ingestion connector broke, or a transformation encoded the wrong definition. "Is the data fresh?" — a freshness question straight out of the observability pillars (Monte Carlo) — is a fair and specific thing to ask when a number looks off.

Second, it makes you a better producer of data, not just a consumer. Your product emits the events at the very first stage of the lifecycle (O'Reilly), so when you rename a field or change an event's shape, you can break a pipeline three stops downstream. That is exactly the schema-change crash data contracts exist to prevent (Monte Carlo) — so flagging instrumentation changes to the data team early is part of your job.

Third, it sharpens your asks. "Can we get this in real time?" means asking for streaming over batch, with real cost implications (Striim). "Can we redefine active user?" is cheap under ELT because raw data is retained (Databricks). And "can we trust this metric?" is really a question about whether the transformation is tested (DataCamp). Speaking the supply-chain language makes every one of these requests land better.

Costs, reliability, and pitfalls

The pitfalls cluster in a few predictable places. The first is brittleness from upstream change. OneUptime warns that source schemas change — "columns get added, renamed, or removed" — which silently breaks pipelines unless engineers handle it defensively. (OneUptime) Much pipeline maintenance is just absorbing changes made by teams who did not know anyone was downstream of them.

The second is bad data propagating. OneUptime's guidance is to validate at two levels — row-level checks (positive amounts, valid formats) and dataset-level checks (row counts, NULL rates, date ranges) — because "Catching bad data early prevents it from propagating through your pipeline and corrupting downstream tables." (OneUptime) One wrong number at ingestion becomes a wrong number in every dashboard built on it.

The third is cost. Streaming runs always-on infrastructure rather than scheduled windows (Striim), and managed ingestion connectors often price by data volume, so a chatty new event can quietly raise the monthly bill. The fourth is the data-downtime trap Monte Carlo names: a pipeline that fails loudly is easy; the dangerous case is one that keeps running while quietly serving partial or wrong data (Monte Carlo). The honest summary: a data platform is a living system that needs the same testing, monitoring, and on-call care as the product itself — which is the whole reason DataOps and reliability practices exist (Airbyte).

Where this connects — adjacent topics

This primer points outward to several neighbors worth a separate look:

  • Data warehouses — the storage-and-analytics destination data engineering fills; OLAP, columnar storage, and dimensional modeling are the companion to this plumbing.
  • SQL fundamentals — the language transformations are written in and the one you would use to query the served tables yourself.
  • Product analytics & instrumentation — the event model and tracking plan that determine what enters the lifecycle at the generation stage.
  • Machine learning for PMs — a major "serving" consumer; ML (Machine Learning) models are fed by the same pipelines, and feature pipelines are a data-engineering specialty.
  • Data governance & privacy — the security and data-management undercurrents: access control, retention, and compliance over everything moving through the pipes.
  • APIs — how data is often extracted from source systems; an API (Application Programming Interface) is a common ingestion entry point alongside change data capture.
pmos Source: docs/pmos/primer/2026-06-14_data-engineering.html · Created using pmos‑learnkit
